coredata.dc:title: A carbonized polymer dot (CPD) nanosensor for trace water detection with a wide detection range
coredata.dc:creator: Huo, Zepeng
Xu, Weiqing
Wang, Zizhun
Xu, Shuping
coredata.dc:description: Water plays an essential role in life activities, but in some scenes, water is impurity. Herein, we developed a fluorescent nanoprobe for tracing water content based on the carbonized polymer dot (CPD) prepared by ethylenediamine and catechol via the one-pot hydrothermal method. This carbon dot shows different responses in different organic solvents due to its abundant surface functional group controlled by hydrogen bonds. This nanoprobe exhibits excellent detection performance for the water content in various organic solvents, especially in 1,4-dioxane. The limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0460 vol% and relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.198% in 0.1 vol% water content (n = 3) were obtained by this analytical method. More importantly, this CPD-based fluorescent analytical method presents the broadest detection range (0.0–100.0 vol%) in tracing water in heavy water compared with other probes, and shows good repeatability (RSD = 2.25%,5.0 vol% water content, n = 3) and low LOD (5.51 vol%). This study shows a new perspective for real-time monitoring water content and expands the applications of carbon materials in analytical fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Surface state
Water content detection
Heavy water
Organic solvent

Detections of water in different organic solvents
Detection of water content in heavy water

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21873039 ), and Interdisciplinary Integration Innovation Project of Jilin University, China (Grant No. JLUXKJC2020106 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and the size distribution (insert) of the CPD. (b) Raman spectrum of the CPD under the excitation of a 532 nm laser. (c) FT-IR spectra of the CPD and catechol. (d) Normalized PL emission spectra of the CPD in different solvents. (e) Photographs of the CPDs in different solvents under the 365 nm UV light. From left to right: toluene, 1,4-dioxane, acetone, dimethyl sulfoxide, isopropanol, ethanol, and water.

(a) Normalized PL emission spectra of the CPD in 1,4-dioxane with different water content. (b) The linear relationship of the PL emission wavelengths of the CPD with the mixed solution of 1,4-dioxane and water. Photographs of the CPD in different solvents under the 365 nm UV light. (I-VI) in the insert of (b) stand for the water proportions of 0.0%, 5.0%, 20.0%, 40.0%, 60.0% and 100.0%, respectively.

(a)–(f) The fitting results of the CPDs for the water contents in different solvents. Fitting formula:

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the CPD in heavy water and water. (b) PL lifetime decay profiles of CPD in H2O and D2O. (c) PL responses of the CPD in heavy water toward different amounts of water, and (d) the linear relationship of (F0–F)/F0 to water content, while F0 and F are the PL intensity of CPD prepared at 510 nm before and after the addition of H2O. Note: IRF indicates the instrumental response function.
Comparison of the performances of CDs used for water content detections in organic solvents.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: #divided into four sections. *Hydrothermal synthesis. EDA: Ethylenediamine; THF: Tetrahydrofuran; ACN: Acetonitrile; DMF: Dimethylformamide; DMSO: Dimethyl sulfoxide.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the performance of other fluorescent probes for water content detection in heavy water.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: a LOD: limit of detection. b FRET: fluorescence resonance energy transfer. c [PCM-22, comprising triphenylphosphine and Ln3+ions (Ln = Pr–Yb)].

head.ce:title: A carbonized polymer dot (CPD) nanosensor for trace water detection with a wide detection range

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Theoretical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water plays an essential role in life activities, but in some scenes, water is impurity. Herein, we developed a fluorescent nanoprobe for tracing water content based on the carbonized polymer dot (CPD) prepared by ethylenediamine and catechol via the one-pot hydrothermal method. This carbon dot shows different responses in different organic solvents due to its abundant surface functional group controlled by hydrogen bonds. This nanoprobe exhibits excellent detection performance for the water content in various organic solvents, especially in 1,4-dioxane. The limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0460 vol% and relative standard deviation (RSD) of 0.198% in 0.1 vol% water content (n = 3) were obtained by this analytical method. More importantly, this CPD-based fluorescent analytical method presents the broadest detection range (0.0–100.0 vol%) in tracing water in heavy water compared with other probes, and shows good repeatability (RSD = 2.25%,5.0 vol% water content, n = 3) and low LOD (5.51 vol%). This study shows a new perspective for real-time monitoring water content and expands the applications of carbon materials in analytical fields.

We successfully developed a novel ultrasensitive fluorescent probe based on carbonized polymer dot (CPD) for water content detection in many environments (D2O and organic solvents).
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Water is extremely indispensable for human beings. However, in some environments, the trace water content brings side effects [1]. For example, many organic reactions would fail due to the water residues. Besides, moisture content control is crucial for drugs, chemicals, and food systems [2]. A small amount of water will cause drug failure and damage the production line and equipment severely. Therefore, developing an effective, fast and convenient water content detection probe is of great value not only in laboratory conditions but also in actual industrial production. The standard trace water analytical methods are Karl-Fischer titration and chromatography. These methods need expensive instruments, complicate sample preprocessing and require highly skilled analysts [3]. In recent years, fluorescent sensing technology has been widely concerned with simple, efficient, and intuitive advantages [4]. At present, the commonly used fluorescent probes for water content are small organic molecules, such as flavone dyes [5], fluorescent 3-hydroxychromones [6], and coumarin conjugate [1] etc. These probes require complicated organic synthesis and purification procedures.

As an important solvent, heavy water (D2O) plays an irreplaceable role in chemical analysis and nuclear reactions [7]. However, due to the slight difference in chemical properties between heavy water and water, it is not easy to separate them [8]. To date, few sensitive detection methods have been reported to distinguish water from heavy water, and available fluorescent probes are rare.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a new luminescent material discovered accidently in 2004 [9]. Compared with the traditional quantum dots, CDs have the advantages of good biocompatibility, good stability, simple synthesis, and low cost [10]. Based on those advantages, CDs have been widely used for lighting display, photovoltaic cells, and catalysis, fluorescent sensing, etc. [11]. Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) is one of the attractive materials in CDs' families. It has a core-shell structure with high carbonization inside and abundant polymer chains or functional groups outside [12]. Rich surface structures endow CPDs with high chemical stability and easy functionalization, shows great performance with sensing responses to metal ions [13], pH [14], and biomolecules [15], based on the quenching effect (“On−Off” strategy) of fluorescence of CDs [16]. There are three mechanisms to explain the PL quenching of CDs, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photo-induced electron transfer (PET), and inner filter effect (IFE). Another sensing strategy is based on the “On−Off−On” regulation [17]. In this strategy, the fluorescence of CDs previously quenched by quenchers can gradually recover with the addition of the probed analytes. Besides the intensity variation, changing the energy level of CDs to make band shifting is another option for the CDs’ sensing [18]. The peak-shifting can also be identified by the visual detections through fluorescence colors, facilitating observation by naked eyes. Regarding water content detections by CDs, to our knowledge, several typically beneficial attempts have been tried in publications, as listed in Table 1 , in which one is the “on-off” strategy, and the other three are the fluorescence band shifting strategies.

This study develops a new fluorescent CPD that can respond to the water content in various solvents, including D2O. The CPD was synthesized by a green hydrothermal method using catechol and ethylenediamine as precursors through dehydration, polymerization, carbonization. The response curves of water content in different organic solvents were discussed, and the ultra-sensitive detection as low as 0.0460% of trace water content in 1,4-dioxane was achieved. This nanoprobe was used to detect water content in heavy water with a wider detection range beyond other water content probes. The sensing mechanism will be discussed, and evidenced experimentally by several photophysical parameters, such as fluorescence lifetime, fluorescence quantum yield, radiative and nonradiative decay rate constant.

CPD was synthesized from catechol (108 mg) and ethylenediamine (120 μL) by one pot hydrothermal method (10.0 mL deionized water). In brief, the mixture was ultrasonicated for 10 min, and then the solution was transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 12 h at 180 °C. After the reaction system was cooled to room temperature, the impurities were removed by filtration by a membrane with a size of 0.22 μm and centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 10 min. Unreacted precursors were removed by dialysis (1000 Da dialysis bag for about 48 h dialysis). Finally, the as-prepared CPDs (freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C) were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of water content in different solvents
ce:section.ce:para: First, we dissolved the solid CPDs in various solvents to prepare 10.0 mg/mL of solutions. After that, 10.0 μL of the above solution were diluted with different proportions of the water/solvent mixtures and then filled to 2.00 mL. After preparing the solution at room temperature for 10 min, the fluorescence spectra were collected by a fluorescence spectrometer (SHIMADZU RF-5301PC) with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Similar experimental conditions were used to detect the water content in heavy water, except that the excitation wavelength was 400 nm.

The as-prepared CPD was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), Raman spectroscopy, and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), respectively. The TEM image presents the monodispersed morphology of the CPD (Fig. 1 a) and gives a particle size of 3.96 ± 0.55 nm. Raman spectroscopy was used to study the internal structure of CPD. It can be observed from Fig. 1b that three Raman bands corresponding to D, G, and 2D peaks can be identified, respectively [22–24]. The intensity ratio of the D peak to G peak can be applied for evaluating the defect degree of carbon materials. For our CPD, a higher ID/IG ratio value is observed, indicating various defects existing in this CPD [25]. The functional groups and chemical bonds on the surface of CPD were studied by FT-IR (Fig. 1c). Compared with catechol, the CPD shows more characteristic absorption peaks (Table S1), e.g., the stretching modes of amino (N–H) and hydroxyl (O–H) absorption peaks (3053 and 3242 cm−1), CO and CC/CN (1670 and 1585 cm−1), the stretching and bending vibrations of methylene (–CH2, 2960, 2962, and 1440 cm−1), and so on. The FT-IR data indicate that though catechol has been converted to the skeleton of the CPD, many functional groups remain on the CPD, such as oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups, which enrich the CPD with rich energy levels and reaction sites for sensing properties.

This CPD has the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CPD at 415 and 510 nm, respectively. The excitation peak coincides with the newly emerging absorption peak of the CPD (Figs. S1a and S1b). It exhibits no excitation-dependence behaviors (Fig. S1b). However, it shows good responsibility to the polarity of solvents. As shown in Fig. 1d, with the gradual increase of polarity, the emission wavelength of the CPD starts red-shifting accordingly. Typically, the maximum emission peak position (λ max) of the CPD in 1,4-dioxane is 438 nm. For water, the λ max of CPD redshifts to 510 nm. Similarly, with the change of polarity, the maximum absorption wavelength is red-shifted gradually (Fig. S2). To understand the law of solvent response more clearly. We used ET(30) (a parameter describing the polarity of solvent) to fit the emission peak [26]. As shown in Table S2 and Fig. S3a, a linear relationship between λ max of fluorescence and ET(30) is obtained (R2 = 0.967). Also, the absorption and emission spectra of the CPD in different alcohol solvents present the same trends (Fig. S4), which can be explained by the formation of hydrogen bonds between the CPD and solvents that stabilize the energy level of the excited state [20,27,28]. These optical characterizations suggest that our CPD can be used as a potential probe for the polarity of solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of water in different organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: Trace water is usually one of the most common impurities in organic solvents [29]. Herein, we applied our CPDs for trace water determination in six kinds of organic solvents, including 1,4-dioxane, acetonitrile, N,N-dimethylformamide, dimethyl sulfoxide, methanol, and ethanol. Fig. 2 a and 2b shows the normalized PL and color changes of the CPD in the 1,4-dioxane/water mixed solution under the 365 nm excitation, and the response gives a linear relation with the water content in the range of 0.0%–1.0% (Fig. 2b).

Inspired by this phenomenon, we measured the fluorescence peak changes when the water contents are 0.0%–100.0% (v/v) in other organic solvents. Fig. 3 plots the relationships between the emission wavelength and the water content in different solvents. It can be found that the CPD exhibits similar fluorescence changing trends in different aprotic solvents, except for two proton solvents (methanol and ethanol). No significant peak shifts of fluorescence emission wavelength were observed when the low contents of water (0.0%–20.0%) were applied to methanol or ethanol. When water content reaches up to 20.0%, the fluorescence emission peak position changes obviously, indicating that CPD could be affected by the hydrogen bonding network, exactly.

The relationships between the response behavior of the CPD in different solvents were built. Two formulas were used to fit the water content change in different solvents at different stages (20.0 %v/v as the cut-off point). Three parameters, y
0 , A
1 , and t
1 , can be obtained, representing the sensitivity of organic solvents to water in aprotic solvents. It can be seen that those values of each organic solvent are related to the solvent characteristic parameter ET(30). For example, in organic solvents with similar polarities, such as DMSO and DMF, A
1 values are close. A lower t
1 can be achieved in low polarity solvents, revealing that CPD has higher sensitivity to the polarity of the solvent (Table S4).

To deeply understand the reasons for the fluorescence changes of CPD in the binary solvent mixture, we selected three solvents (1,4-dioxane, N,N-dimethylformamide and ethanol) and water mixed systems, calculated the solvation factors (η) of the three by using equations (1)–(3) [30–32].
(1)
E p
= − 0.492
E T N
+ 2.90
(2)
E T N
(
m i x
)
=
E
T
( m )
−
E
T
( O S )
E
T
( W )
−
E
T
( O S )
(3)
η =
E
T 1
( m )
χ
w 2
E
T 2
( m )
χ
w 1

In the above equation, E
p is the energy corresponding to the maximum peak position of fluorescence emission of CPD in different solvents. The normalized polarity parameter of the solvents can be described as as E
T
N (Table S2). χ
W is the molar fraction of water. Molar electronic transition energy (kcal mol−1) for organic solvents and water were expressed as E
T(OS) and E
T(W) (Table S2). E
T1(m) and E
T2(m) are molar electronic transition energy obtained at two different molar fractions of the water/organic solvent, respectively (Fig. S5). In addition, it is worth noting that equation (1) obtained by scattering fitting in Fig. S3b shows a linear relation (R2 = 0.957), indicating that above results can be extended to binary mixed solutions [30].

In a binary solvent mixture, the E
T(m) value increases nonlinearly with the change of molar fraction of water (Fig. S5), which is inconsistent with the theoretical value, suggesting the occurrence of preferential solvation of different polar components in the binary solvent mixture. We interpolated the curves obtained from three groups and evaluated the values of solvation factors (η) in several typical regions. As shown in Fig. S5, the solvation factors in the three solvent/water mixed systems show entirely different trends. For example, in the 1,4-dioxane/water system, each section shows different preferential solvation processes, and the water content molar fraction of 0.6 is the dividing point. In addition, the experimental value shows the most significant deviation from the theoretical value when the water molar fraction arrives at 0.2. The DMF/water mixed system is similar to the 1,4-dioxane/water one, except a different η turning point is obtained. On the contrary, in the low water content region of the ethanol/water system, the solvation factor is about 1, which may be due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between CPDs and ethanol. In conclusion, in the low water content region, water as a higher polar component preferentially solvates CPD, while organic solvents as a lower polar component preferentially solvate CPD in the high water content region. More importantly, the linear relationship in the region of low water content (the molar fraction of water is about 0.5) in ethanol indicates the interaction between CPD and solvent, especially hydrogen bond. The above results confirm that our CPD is preferentially solvated by different polar components in the binary solvent mixtures, which explains the reason for two-stage fitting results [19,20].

The relationship between the fluorescence emission peak and water content in the linear ranges shows the results in Figs. 2b, S8b, S9b, and S10b. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated by 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and s is the slope of the calibration curve in the linear response region [33]. The calculated LODs are 0.0460, 0.178, 1.10, and 1.25 vol% for 1,4-dioxane, acetonitrile, DMF, and DMSO. For protonic solvents, the water content detection performances give wide linear ranges (Fig. S11b and S12b, 0.0-40.0 %v/v for methanol, 0.0–10.0 %v/v for ethanol). The LODs are 7.06 and 5.38 vol% for methanol and ethanol. Compared with other water content sensing materials (Table 1), this CPD possesses better sensing performance, especially in 1,4-dioxane. A performance comparison table for water content detection of 1,4-dioxane is also listed in Table S5. We used the relative standard deviation (RSD) value to evaluate the reliability of the developed sensing method. The results show that the CPD has good repeatability in the presence of 0.1 vol% water content in 1,4-dioxane (RSD = 0.198%). On the other hand, our CPD also exhibits good reproducibility with intra-day and inter-day, due to the RSDs being 0.275% (n = 3) and 0.264% (n = 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in heavy water
ce:section.ce:para: Heavy water is easily affected by moisture in the air, resulting in deliquescence and pollution, which affects the quality of heavy water [34]. We applied our nanoprobe for tracing water in heavy water. We first compared the absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of the CPD in heavy water and water. It can be seen that the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the CPD in both of them have no identifiable changes (Fig. S13), except the absorption spectrum of the CPD in heavy water shows a new peak at 335 nm compared with that in water (Fig. 4 a) and the 279 nm peak increases as well. We guess the different behaviors of our CPD in heavy water and water might be explained by different protonation and deprotonation degrees of hydroxyl groups of the CPD in different pH values [pH (H2O) = 6.4–7.0, but pD (D2O) = 7.5] [8]. The pH-responsive feature of the CPD has been well studied in many pieces of literature including ours [35–37]. The pH variation from 7.0 to 7.5 only brings slight changes in the absorption band and fluorescence intensity (weakens, Fig. S15). However, the absorption and fluorescence profiles give more distinct changes between D2O and H2O (Fig. 4a and c). We speculate that the pH difference between the D2O and H2O is not the only reason for our CPD response, and there might be other reasons behind it.

To explore the reason for this phenomenon, we further measured the fluorescence lifetimes, and the PL decay curves of CPD in H2O and D2O, as shown in Fig. 4b. It displays a bi-exponential function of H2O and D2O, and the fitted lifetimes are 3.74 ns (94.6%), 4.81 ns (59.0%) for the short lifetime components, and 9.22 ns (5.4%), 10.00 ns (41.0%) for the larger lifetime components, respectively. Nevertheless, the presence of H2O triggers a shorter lifetime than D2O. Further, the averaged lifetime of CPD in H2O and D2O is calculated as 4.03 ns and 6.94 ns, respectively. This can be assigned to the higher quenching efficacy of O–H oscillators relative to O-D at the excited states [8]. Besides, the absolute fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of CPD in heavy water and water were measured. Compared with water, CPD showed increased QY in heavy water (Table S6). These QY results also show that water has a stronger quenching effect on our CPD than D2O has. As shown in Tables S8 and S9 and Fig. S14, with the increase of water content in heavy water, the fluorescence lifetime becomes shorter and the fluorescence quantum yields decreases, accompanied by the apparent increase of k
nr and the unchanged k
r , which can be attributed to the response of surface states to different solvents and be consistent with those reported in previous publications [38]. The above results further prove the high quenching efficiency of O–H and implied possible sensing performance. As we discussed before, FT-IR data indicate that our CPD has rich oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups. The CPD containing one or more hydrogen atoms will be sensitive to hydrogen-deuterium exchange reactions, and hydrogen atoms can be affected by isotope replacement. Therefore, we speculate the N–H and O–H in the amide, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of our CPD surface can be converted to the N-D and O-D bonds. Thus, the nonradiative decay is likely to be affected by the vibrational mode frequencies involved in electron energy dissipation, such as stretching and bending [38].

Owing to the dual effect of the pH and the H-D exchange on the surface groups of our CPD, we expect this CPD can work as a novel fluorescent nanoprobe for water content detection in heavy water. Thus, we measured the PL spectra of the CPD in heavy water involving different contents of water. As shown in Fig. 4c, the PL intensity of CPD decreases with the increase of H2O content in heavy water. The fluorescence intensity is proportional to H2O content in the range of 0.0–100.0% vol%, and the LOD is 5.51 vol% (3σ). The RSD of 5.0 vol% H2O content (n = 3) is 2.25%, indicating that the results obtained by this method are reliable. Surprisingly, this CPD nanoprobe enables the broadest water content range in the reported water/heavy water sensing systems (Table 2 ). This indicates that the dual effect involving the pH and the H-D exchange affects the excited states of the CPD, which is conducive to the water content sensing performance.

In summary, we developed a high-performance, carbon-based, water-content fluorescent nanoprobe by the one-step synthesis method. The surface state-controlled sensing mechanism were discussed by analyzing a variety of photophysical parameters. The luminescence of CPD in organic solvent/water system is affected by solvent polarity and solvent characteristics (such as hydrogen bond). For the heavy water/water system, the co-contribution of the protonation/deprotonation, the isotope exchange for the CPD's surface functional groups and energy dissipation relating process answer for the sensing mechanism. This nanoprobe can be employed for detecting trace water in aprotic solvents at a low level and giving the broadest detection range in determining water content in heavy water. This work broadens the applications of CDs for detecting water in various environments. It is also another valuable attempt to design carbon-based fluorescent nanosensors according to the regulation of surface states.

Zepeng Huo: conceived the idea, designed the experiments, analyzed data, and wrote the manuscript. Weiqing Xu: conceived the idea, designed the experiments, and analyzed data. Zizhun Wang: participated the experiments and data analysis of transmission electron microscope (TEM). Shuping Xu: analyzed data, wrote and revised the manuscript as a supervisor. The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21873039), and Interdisciplinary Integration Innovation Project of Jilin University, China (Grant No. JLUXKJC2020106).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109805.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The coumarin conjugate: synthesis, photophysical properties and the ratiometric fluorescence response to water content of organic solvent

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang KP, Lei Y, Chen JP, Ge ZH, Liu W, Zhang Q, et al. The coumarin conjugate: Synthesis, photophysical properties and the ratiometric fluorescence response to water content of organic solvent. Dyes Pigments 2018;151:233-237.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Double-color lanthanide metal-organic framework based logic device and visual ratiometric fluorescence water microsensor for solid pharmaceuticals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yu L, Zheng Q, Wang H, Liu C, Huang X, Xiao Y. Double-color lanthanide metal-organic framework based logic device and visual ratiometric fluorescence water microsensor for solid pharmaceuticals. Anal Chem 2020;92:1402-1408.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Negative solvatochromism of merocyanine dyes: application as water content probes for organic solvents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cha S, Choi MG, Jeon HR, Chang SK. Negative solvatochromism of merocyanine dyes: Application as water content probes for organic solvents. Sensor Actuator B Chem 2011;157:14-18.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for the detection of humidity or water content

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jung HS, Verwilst P, Kim WY, Kim JS. Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for the detection of humidity or water content. Chem Soc Rev 2016;45:1242-1256.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvatochromogenic flavone dyes for the detection of water in acetone

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu W, Wang Y, Jin W, Shen G, Yu R. Solvatochromogenic flavone dyes for the detection of water in acetone. Anal Chim Acta 1999;383:299-307.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive fluorescent probe for the hydrophobic range of solvent polarities

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ercelen S, Klymchenko AS, Demchenko AP. Ultrasensitive fluorescent probe for the hydrophobic range of solvent polarities. Anal Chim Acta 2002;464:273-287.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-site controlled fluorescent sensor for the facile and fast detection of H2O in D2O by two turn-on emission signals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dong B, Lu Y, Song W, Kong X, Sun Y, Lin W. A dual-site controlled fluorescent sensor for the facile and fast detection of H2O in D2O by two turn-on emission signals. Chem Commun 2020;56:1191-1194.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile strategy for the construction of purely organic optical sensors capable of distinguishing D2O from H2O

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Luo Y, Li C, Zhu W, Zheng X, Huang Y, Lu Z. A facile strategy for the construction of purely organic optical sensors capable of distinguishing D2O from H2O. Angew Chem Int Ed. 2019;58:6280-6284.
